description:
<title>Configure shell environment variable for incident response</title>
    <description>
        You are an on-call incident responder who has just SSHed into a freshly-provisioned Linux host.  
        Security policy requires that every responder enables a special environment flag so that future
        interactive shells automatically load incident-specific tooling.

        Objective:
        • Append an “export” line to your personal Bash start-up file so that *all future* interactive
          sessions inherit the variable INCIDENT_RESPONDER with the value active.

        Technical requirements that the automated grader will check:

        1. File to modify: /home/user/.bashrc  
           • A single new line must be added to the very end of the file (after any existing content).  
           • That line must match *exactly* (including case, quotes and spacing):  
             export INCIDENT_RESPONDER="active"
           • The line must be terminated by a single Unix newline character (LF).

        2. Confirmation log: /home/user/ir_run.log  
           • After the configuration is done, create (or overwrite) this log file.  
           • The file must contain one and only one line, with this exact text:  
             INCIDENT_RESPONDER variable configured successfully  
           • Also terminated by a single Unix newline character (LF).

        Constraints:
        • Complete everything using just ONE compound shell command executed from your current terminal
          session. You MAY use shell operators (e.g., “&&”, “;”, redirection) inside that single command.
        • Do not open an editor interactively; achieve the change non-interactively.
        • No sudo or elevated privileges are available or required because you are writing inside your
          own home directory.

        The grader will:
        • Read the final two lines of /home/user/.bashrc and expect the exact export statement as shown.
        • Check that /home/user/ir_run.log exists and contains the precise success message.
        • Verify that only one newline terminates each file and that no extra spaces are present.

        When you are finished, you can run:
            tail -n 2 /home/user/.bashrc
            cat /home/user/ir_run.log
        to visually confirm your work, but this is optional.
    </description>
truth:
<final_state>
        <file path="/home/user/.bashrc">
export INCIDENT_RESPONDER="active"
</file>
        <file path="/home/user/ir_run.log">INCIDENT_RESPONDER variable configured successfully
</file>
    </final_state>
